refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks clarity on the mechanisms by which efficiency is achieved. Refinement introduces the key enablers: monitoring (telemetry), control (operator/tele-operator actions), and feedback loops. This makes the requirement testable and decomposable into sub-functions that map to system capabilities.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over the evaluation period. Compare against E_target. Verification requires instrumentation (energy sensors, load sensors, distance/work tracking) and data aggregation across all operational modes (manual, tele-operated, autonomous if applicable).
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved, distance, cycle count) via telemetry.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy efficiency feedback to the operator/tele-operator to enable informed control decisions.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall optimize powertrain and auxiliary system operation to minimize energy per unit work output.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy and work metrics for post-shift and trend analysis by Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that monitors real-time power draw, calculates efficiency metrics, and sends feedback to operator HMI; couple with powertrain control algorithms that optimize gear selection, engine throttle, and auxiliary loads based on work demand.

DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring across vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, electrical) with local optimization logic; aggregate data at a central telemetry hub for reporting and tele-operator visibility.
DP-C: Implement passive energy monitoring (sensors only) with post-hoc analysis; rely on operator training and manual control optimization without real-time feedback.

DP-D: Integrate energy efficiency as a constraint in a model-predictive control (MPC) framework that optimizes trajectory, speed, and load distribution in real-time, coupled with tele-operator override capability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring across vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, electrical) with local optimization logic; aggregate data at a central telemetry hub for reporting and tele-operator visibility.

potential_sources: Coupling between powertrain control (engine, transmission) and hydraulic system optimization—mitigated by clear energy accounting boundaries and local control loops.
Coupling between real-time feedback (operator HMI) and tele-operator command latency—mitigated by asynchronous telemetry and local autonomy in subsystem control.
Coupling between energy optimization and safety constraints (e.g., braking, stability)—mitigated by safety-critical override logic that takes precedence over efficiency optimization.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B avoids over-centralization (DP-A risk: single point of failure) and passive monitoring (DP-C risk: no real-time feedback). DP-D is deferred as it introduces complexity (MPC) not yet justified by requirements. DP-B allows modular subsystem optimization while maintaining system-level visibility.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, electrical) have sufficient instrumentation (sensors) to measure power draw. Risk: Legacy equipment may lack sensors; mitigation requires retrofit or sensor integration plan.

Assumption: Tele-operator network link has sufficient bandwidth and latency (<500ms) to support real-time feedback. Risk: Poor network conditions degrade feedback utility; mitigation: graceful degradation to local operator feedback.
Assumption: Operator and tele-operator training includes energy efficiency awareness. Risk: Without training, feedback is ignored; mitigation: mandatory training and incentive alignment.

Assumption: Energy optimization does not conflict with safety-critical functions. Risk: Efficiency-driven control (e.g., reduced braking power) could compromise safety; mitigation: safety override logic and validation testing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-B is sound. Recommend explicit definition of subsystem optimization algorithms (e.g., engine throttle mapping, hydraulic pressure optimization) and safety override logic in the next decomposition level. Also clarify the telemetry aggregation protocol and data freshness requirements.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module encompasses all on-board energy monitoring, subsystem control, and local optimization logic. It includes engine management, hydraulic system control, electrical system management, and the on-board telemetry aggregation unit. The Vehicle module is the primary owner of energy efficiency realization.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sends real-time energy consumption (kWh), work output (tons, distance), efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton), and subsystem status to the NGHE Solution and Teleoperation modules for operator feedback and logging.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives operator/tele-operator commands (speed, direction, load control, mode selection) from the Teleoperation module and translates them into subsystem control setpoints.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints and override signals from the Safety Services module; sends safety-critical alerts (e.g., energy system faults, thermal limits) back to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle communicates with Teleoperation and NGHE Solution via Network Link for telemetry upload and command reception.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator and would own energy efficiency KPI tracking and reporting. However, the actual energy monitoring and optimization logic resides in Vehicle. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse the core energy management responsibility across multiple modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), violating cohesion. Not recommended as primary IM.

Vehicle is the natural owner of energy monitoring (sensors, data aggregation) and subsystem optimization (engine, hydraulics, electrical control). All energy-related hardware and control logic are physically and functionally contained within the vehicle. Telemetry export is a clean external interface. High containment, low diffusion. Recommended.

Teleoperation is responsible for remote command and feedback presentation, not energy management. Assigning energy optimization to Teleoperation would scatter control logic across remote and on-board systems, creating latency and synchronization issues. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety constraints and override logic, not energy optimization. While energy efficiency must respect safety limits, the primary energy management responsibility should not reside in Safety Services. Safety Services provides constraints; Vehicle respects them. Not recommended as primary IM.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module contains all necessary hardware (sensors, actuators, control units) and software (monitoring, optimization algorithms, telemetry aggregation) to realize DP-B. Energy monitoring and subsystem optimization are inherently vehicle-centric functions. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, indicating strong cohesion within Vehicle and clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle module may become overloaded if additional energy-related features (e.g., predictive maintenance, machine learning optimization) are added without careful interface design. Mitigation: Define clear sub-module boundaries within Vehicle (e.g., Engine Control Unit, Hydraulic Control Unit, Telemetry Aggregator) and manage their interfaces.

Risk: Telemetry data freshness and accuracy depend on sensor calibration and network reliability. If sensors drift or network latency increases, energy_per_ton calculation becomes unreliable. Mitigation: Implement sensor health monitoring and data validation logic within Vehicle; define acceptable latency bounds for Telemetry interface.

Risk: Safety override logic in Vehicle must be fail-safe. If energy optimization inadvertently compromises safety (e.g., reduced braking), the system fails the safety criticality requirement. Mitigation: Formal verification of safety override logic; safety-critical testing before deployment.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Vehicle is the correct IM boundary. Recommend further decomposition of Vehicle into sub-modules (Engine Control, Hydraulic Control, Electrical Control, Telemetry Aggregator) in the next level to manage complexity and clarify internal interfaces. Also recommend explicit definition of the Telemetry interface schema (data fields, update frequency, accuracy requirements) and SafetyAlert override protocol.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (distributed monitoring with local optimization and central aggregation) directly addresses the refined FR (enable energy efficiency achievement through monitoring, feedback, and optimization). The DP is implementable, testable, and aligns with the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No independence violations detected; the DP does not over-constrain or under-specify the FR.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface carries energy data from Vehicle to NGHE Solution and Teleoperation. If Telemetry schema is not well-defined, multiple modules may interpret energy metrics differently, causing inconsistency. Mitigation: Standardize Telemetry data format and semantics at the NGHE Solution level.

Tasking interface carries operator commands to Vehicle. If command semantics are ambiguous (e.g., 'maximize efficiency' vs. 'minimize time'), Vehicle may not optimize correctly. Mitigation: Define clear command semantics and energy optimization priorities in the Tasking interface specification.

SafetyAlert interface enforces safety overrides. If safety constraints are not clearly communicated to Vehicle, energy optimization may inadvertently violate safety limits. Mitigation: Formal specification of safety constraints and override logic; safety-critical testing.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module contains the core energy management logic and is the single owner of energy monitoring and subsystem optimization. External interfaces are minimal and well-scoped. No significant diffusion of energy management responsibility across modules. Containment is strong.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-003 requires measurement of energy_per_ton over the evaluation period. Vehicle module provides Telemetry interface with energy consumption and work output data. NGHE Solution aggregates this data and compares against E_target. Verification is feasible if Telemetry data is accurate and complete. Recommend defining acceptance test procedures (e.g., controlled worksite trials, energy measurement protocols) in the next level.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CR-NGHE-003 is allocated to Vehicle module via DP-B. Sub-FRs (monitoring, feedback, optimization, logging) are clearly mapped to Vehicle subsystems and interfaces. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Define Telemetry interface schema in detail: data fields (energy_consumed, work_output, efficiency_metrics), update frequency, accuracy requirements, and error handling.
Specify energy optimization algorithms and priorities: e.g., minimize energy_per_ton subject to safety and performance constraints. Define how operator/tele-operator commands influence optimization.
Formalize safety override logic: define which safety constraints take precedence over energy optimization and how overrides are triggered and logged.
Plan sensor retrofit or integration for legacy equipment to ensure energy monitoring capability across the fleet.
Define acceptance test procedures for energy efficiency verification: controlled worksite trials, measurement protocols, data aggregation methods.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 has been successfully refined into a coherent FR with clear DP and IM assignment. The refined FR is non-atomic and has four sub-FRs that require further decomposition. The Vehicle module is the correct IM boundary with strong containment and minimal diffusion. However, the sub-FRs (monitoring, feedback, optimization, logging) are sufficiently complex and distinct that they warrant detailed decomposition in the next level. Proceeding to decompose sub-FRs will clarify subsystem responsibilities, interface specifications, and implementation details.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR is decomposed into four sub-FRs: (1) continuous monitoring and reporting, (2) real-time feedback, (3) powertrain optimization, and (4) logging and aggregation. Each sub-FR maps to distinct Vehicle subsystems (sensors, HMI, control algorithms, data storage) and requires detailed design. Decomposing these sub-FRs will produce concrete DPs and IMs for each subsystem, enabling detailed design and implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved, distance, cycle count) via telemetry.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy efficiency feedback to the operator/tele-operator to enable informed control decisions.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall optimize powertrain and auxiliary system operation to minimize energy per unit work output.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy and work metrics for post-shift and trend analysis by Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems have sufficient instrumentation (sensors) to measure power draw and work output.
Tele-operator network link has sufficient bandwidth and latency (<500ms) to support real-time feedback.
Operator and tele-operator training includes energy efficiency awareness and incentive alignment.
Energy optimization does not conflict with safety-critical functions; safety overrides take precedence.
Energy efficiency is measured as energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over the evaluation period.
E_target is customer-defined and communicated to the system before evaluation begins.
open_risks: Legacy equipment may lack sensors; retrofit or sensor integration plan required.
Poor network conditions may degrade real-time feedback utility; graceful degradation strategy needed.
Operator/tele-operator may ignore efficiency feedback if not properly trained or incentivized.
Energy optimization algorithms may inadvertently compromise safety; formal verification and safety-critical testing required.
Telemetry data accuracy depends on sensor calibration and maintenance; sensor health monitoring and validation logic required.
Energy optimization priorities may conflict with operator preferences or worksite constraints; clear command semantics and priority definition needed.
evidence_needed: Sensor specifications and calibration procedures for energy and work output measurement.
Network latency and bandwidth measurements for Telemetry and Tasking interfaces.
Energy optimization algorithm specifications and performance validation data.
Safety override logic formal specification and verification results.
Operator/tele-operator training materials and competency assessment procedures.
Acceptance test procedures and controlled worksite trial results demonstrating energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.
